classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

title: Chapter XXIV.

source.title: ANF06. Fathers of the Third Century: Gregory Thaumaturgus, Dionysius the Great, Julius Africanus, Anatolius, and Minor Writers, Methodius, Arnobius

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: The judges said: Tell us, O Manes, who designated the boundaries for the kingdom of each, and who made the middle wall?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For Archelaus begs that due importance be attached to the practice of interrogation in this discussion.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Manes said: The God who is good, and who has nothing in common with evil, placed the firmament in the midst, in order to make it plain that the wicked one is an alien to Him.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Archelaus said: How fearfully you belie the dignity of that name!

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: You do indeed call Him God, but you do so in name only, and you make His deity resemble man’s infirmities.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: At one time out of the non-existent, and at another time out of underlying matter, which indeed thus existed before Himself, you assert that He did build the structure, as builders among men are wont to do.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Sometimes also you speak of Him as apprehensive, and sometimes as variable.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: It is, however, the part of God to do what is proper to God, and it is the part of man to do what is proper to man.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: If, then, God, as you say, has constructed a wall, this is a God who marks Himself out as apprehensive, and as possessed of no fortitude.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For we know that it is always the case that those who are suspicious of the preparation of secret perils against them by strangers, and who are afraid of the plots of enemies, are accustomed to surround their cities with walls, by which procedure they at once secure themselves in their ignorance, and display their feeble capacity.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But here, too, we have something which ought not to be passed over by us in silence, but rather brought prominently forward; so that even by the great abundance of our declarations on the subject our adversary’s manifold craftiness may be brought to nought, with the help of the truth on our side.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: We may grant, then, that the structure of the wall has been made with the purpose of serving to distinguish between the two kingdoms; for without this one division it is impossible for either of them to have his own proper kingdom.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But granting this, then it follows further that in the same manner it will also be impossible for the wicked one to pass without his own proper limits and invade the territories of the good King, inasmuch as the wall stands there as an obstacle, unless it should chance first to be cast down, for we have heard that such things have been done by enemies, and indeed with our own eyes we have quite recently seen an achievement of that nature successfully carried out.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: And when a king attacks a citadel surrounded by a strong wall, he uses first of all the ballista and projectiles; then he endeavours to cut through the gates with axes, and to demolish the walls by the battering-rams; and when he at last obtains an entrance, and gains possession of the place, he does whatever he listeth, whether it be his pleasure to carry off the citizens into captivity, or to make a complete destruction of the fortress and its contents, or whether, on the other hand, it may be his will to grant indulgence to the captured stronghold on the humble suit of the conquered.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: What, then, does my opponent here say to this analogy?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Did no adversary substantially—which is as much as to say, designedly—overthrow the muniment cast up between the two?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For in his former statements he has avouched that the darkness passed without its own limits, and supervened upon the kingdom of the good God.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Who, then, overthrew that munition before the one could thus have crossed over to the other?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For it was impossible for the evil one to find any entrance while the munition stood fast.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Why are you silent?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Why do you hesitate, Manichæus?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Yet, although you may hold back, I shall proceed with the task of my own accord.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For if we suppose you to say that God destroyed it, then I have to ask what moved Him in this way to demolish the very thing which He had Himself previously constructed on account of the importunity of the wicked one, and for the purpose of preserving the separation between them?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: In what fit of passion, or under what sense of injury, did He thus set about contending against Himself?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Or was it that He lusted after some of the possessions of the wicked one?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But if none of these things formed the real cause that led God to destroy those very things which He had constructed a long time before with the view of estranging and separating the wicked one from Him, then it must needs be considered no matter of surprise if God should also have become delighted with his society; for, on your supposition, the munition which had been set up with the purpose of securing God against trouble from him, will appear to have been removed just because now he is to be regarded no more as an enemy, but as a friend.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: And, on the other hand, if you aver that the wall was destroyed by the wicked one, tell us then how it can be possible for the works of the good God to be mastered by the wicked one.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For if that is possible, then the evil nature will be proved to be stronger than God.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Furthermore, how can that being, seeing that he is pure and total darkness, surprise the light and apprehend it, while the evangelist gives us the testimony that “the light shineth in darkness, and the darkness comprehended it not?”

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: How is this blind one armed?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: How does the darkness fight against the kingdom of light?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For even as the creatures of God here cannot take in the rays of the sun with uninjured eye, so neither can that being bear the clear vision of the kingdom of light, but he remains for ever a stranger to it, and an alien.
